items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Once again I’m plagued
by vague misgivings...
Shpak’s missing a tape recorder,
and the ambassador’s missing a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core comic effect is the mock-heroic/highbrow phrase «терзают смутные сомнения» applied to petty theft logic, plus the parallel structure «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» meaning each has LOST the item (not possesses it). The actual translation preserves the elevated phrasing (“plagued by vague misgivings”) and correctly encodes loss via “missing,” keeping the detective-like linkage and callback. Rhythm and parallelism are close enough for subtitle comedy.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—very pleased.
Very pleased—hello, Tsar.

Tsar—very pleased.
Hello, Tsar—very pleased!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The humor is Bunsha’s drunken, Soviet-ritual politeness and repetitive self-introduction as “tsar,” clashing with the historical setting. The translation keeps the repetition and awkward politeness, and keeps “Tsar” as the repeated label, so the comic mechanism largely survives. However, it slightly reduces the “I’m the Tsar” self-identifying feel (more like addressing the other as Tsar), which can blur the joke that he’s introducing himself with a title like a name.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference and fully preserves the abrupt authoritarian register and comic escalation.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the reference; preserves the archaic/high-register melancholy and the insulting mismatch with the modern setting.

items.translation:
They’ve walled me in!
Walled me in, demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning and comic paranoia are preserved; “walled me in” is the right image for «замуровали». Minor nuance: dropping “the” before “demons” slightly weakens the sense that “demons” are the agent/subject in his worldview, but it remains clear and performative.

items.translation: So that’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: The line is a mock-solemn religious exclamation; the reference leans into elevated proclamation (“Behold the power…”). The actual translation is accurate and understandable, but slightly more casual/observational (“So that’s what… can do!”), reducing the grandiloquent, sermon-like punch that drives the joke in an atheist/Soviet context.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The comedic value is the Soviet emcee/MC formula (“Everyone’s dancing!” as a set phrase) transplanted into a tsarist feast; the reference recreates that as a club/ballroom announcement. “Everybody dance!” conveys the basic imperative but loses the idiomatic emcee feel and the sense of a formal announcement opening the dance floor; it reads more like a direct command. Joke still works, but flatter.

items.translation: Well, this is my lucky day!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: The line is a breezy, self-satisfied quip by a burglar who has “dropped in” at exactly the right time; it’s about the fortuitous visit/stop. “Well, this is my lucky day!” captures the opportunistic satisfaction and is idiomatic in English, though it slightly shifts from “good timing/stop” to generalized luck. Comic intent is preserved.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar!

Red caviar!

Exotic delicacy—eggplant spread!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Key requirement: do NOT call eggplant spread “caviar,” and preserve the comic escalation/inversion (luxury caviar vs humble eggplant presented as exotic). The translation succeeds on the main constraint (“eggplant spread”), and keeps the punchy reveal. Minor loss: it drops the self-correcting build (“…Yes! Overseas…”) and the rhythm of the famous line, but the joke remains clear.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
is all gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestically made cigarette cases,
a suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The humor is the status-item inventory with escalating counts and the delayed, slightly confused “...three” for the suede jacket. The translation preserves the list structure, the “domestic” qualifier, and the final “—three” tag on the jacket, so the comedic bookkeeping survives. Minor stylistic issue: “domestically made cigarette cases” is a bit heavy/technical for the character’s breathless complaining cadence; “domestic” alone would better match the original’s clipped self-importance.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And you’ll be cured too!

And I’ll be cured too!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: The comedic effect is the calm, sing-song resignation and parallel repetition about being sent to psychiatric care. The translation keeps the repetition and meaning. Minor issue: switching between “they’ll cure you” and “you’ll be cured” slightly weakens the rhythmic parallelism; keeping the same construction each time would better preserve the chant-like quality.

summary.interpretation: 1.09 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.22 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
